sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Muscle strains are common in lifting, sprinting, climbing, and awkward workplace tasks. Management should protect the tissue early without driving excessive rest or premature heavy loading.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sudden localised pain during effort or stretch
- Tenderness, protective spasm, bruising, or reduced force
- Pain with active contraction or lengthening
- Limping or reduced task tolerance depending on the muscle involved

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider avulsion, tendon rupture, fracture, compartment syndrome, or referred pain if the story or severity is atypical
- Large visible bruising, major loss of force, or a palpable defect increases concern for significant injury
- Return to heavy work should be based on function rather than pain alone

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Identify mechanism, location, and whether safe ambulation or light use is possible
- Use relative rest, compression, protected movement, and simple symptom relief within scope
- Escalate if severe swelling, inability to bear weight, or diagnostic uncertainty suggests a more serious injury

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Grade severity from mild load-related pain to major strength loss or function failure
- Introduce staged reloading, isometrics, range restoration, and later energy-storage demands where relevant
- Address flexibility, warm-up, fatigue, and job-specific triggers before return to unrestricted duties
- Refer for imaging or specialist review when rupture, avulsion, or repeated reinjury is suspected

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Document the job or sport demand that triggered the injury and what duties are safe immediately afterward
